skill.name: kegg-database
skill.description: Direct access to KEGG via the REST API for academic-only pathway/gene/compound/drug queries; use when you need precise HTTP-level control or targeted KEGG ID mapping.
skill.summary: Python wrapper for the KEGG REST API to query pathways, genes, compounds, drugs, and perform ID mapping for academic bioinformatics research.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: AI evaluation dismissed all 203 static findings as false positives. The 13 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are bioinformatics identifiers (KEGG pathway codes, EC numbers) not cryptography. The 157 'shell backtick execution' findings are Markdown code formatting and Python examples, not shell commands. The 'path traversal' finding at kegg_api.py:244 is Python string slicing. The 'hardcoded IP' findings are Enzyme Commission numbers (ec:1.1.1.1). All 'hardcoded URL' findings point to the legitimate KEGG academic bioinformatics service (rest.kegg.jp). The skill's actual network calls use Python's standard urllib to query the KEGG REST API, which is its documented and intended purpose. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or credential theft detected. No prompt injection attempts found. Risk is limited to legitimate API access to an academic database.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged bioinformatics identifiers (KEGG pathway codes like map00010, EC numbers like ec:1.1.1.1, organism codes) as 'weak cryptographic algorithms'. These are standard KEGG database identifiers, not cryptographic operations. No encryption, hashing, or cryptographic functions exist in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner confused bioinformatics identifiers with cryptographic algorithms. No cryptographic code exists in any file.

low_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged Markdown inline code formatting (text between backtick characters) as shell backtick command execution. All occurrences are within Markdown documents (SKILL.md, references/kegg_reference.md) as code formatting for KEGG API endpoint syntax and Python code examples. No actual shell execution occurs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner misinterpreted Markdown code formatting backticks as shell execution backticks. The files are documentation, not shell scripts.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded IP address (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'ec:1.1.1.1' as a hardcoded IP address. This is an Enzyme Commission (EC) number for Alcohol Dehydrogenase, a standard bioinformatics classification identifier, not a network IP address.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: EC numbers follow the format ec:X.X.X.X which matches IP address patterns. The context clearly shows these are enzyme classification numbers.

low_findings.title: Path traversal sequence (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged line 244 of kegg_api.py for 'path traversal'. The code is Python string slicing '[:200]' used to truncate output in an example usage block (if __name__ == '__main__'). This is not path traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: String slicing syntax [:200] was misinterpreted by the scanner. The code truncates a string for display in a demo block.

low_findings.title: Hard link creation (false positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged line 71 of SKILL.md for 'hard link creation'. The code is a Python generator expression using splitlines() in a Markdown code example. It does not create filesystem links.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Python string method splitlines() was misinterpreted as filesystem link creation. The code is within a Python example block.

content.user_title: Query KEGG pathways, genes, compounds and drugs via REST API
content.value_statement: Bioinformatics researchers need programmatic access to KEGG databases for pathway analysis and gene mapping. This skill provides direct Python wrappers for the KEGG REST API with support for info, list, find, get, convert, link, and drug-drug interaction operations.
actual_capabilities: Retrieve KEGG database metadata and statistics using kegg_info
Search for entries by keyword or molecular properties with kegg_find
Fetch detailed entries including sequences, structures, and pathway maps using kegg_get
Convert identifiers between KEGG and external databases (UniProt, NCBI, PubChem) with kegg_conv
Cross-reference entries across KEGG databases (gene to pathway, pathway to compound) with kegg_link
Look up drug-drug interactions for KEGG drug IDs with kegg_ddi
limitations: KEGG REST API is restricted to academic use only; commercial use requires a separate license
Batch operations are limited to 10 entries per request; pathway images, KGML, and JSON are single-entry only
KEGG does not publish official rate limits, so aggressive polling may result in rate limiting
Output formats are raw text from the KEGG API and require parsing by the caller

use_cases.title: Gene to pathway mapping for enrichment analysis
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics researcher can find pathways associated with a list of differentially expressed genes by using kegg_link to map gene IDs to pathway IDs, then retrieve pathway details with kegg_get.

use_cases.title: Drug-drug interaction screening
use_cases.description: A pharmacologist can check known drug-drug interactions for a set of KEGG drug IDs using kegg_ddi, supporting medication safety analysis in drug development pipelines.

use_cases.title: Cross-database identifier conversion
use_cases.description: A computational biologist can convert between KEGG gene IDs and external database identifiers (UniProt, NCBI Gene, PubChem) using kegg_conv, enabling integration across multiple bioinformatics data sources.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologist working on multi-omics data integration

prompt_templates.title: Find pathways for a human gene
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the kegg-database skill to find which KEGG pathways contain the human gene TP53 (hsa:7157). First use kegg_link to map the gene to pathways, then use kegg_get to retrieve the details of the first pathway returned.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: A researcher wants to understand which biological pathways a specific gene participates in.

prompt_templates.title: Search for compounds by molecular formula
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the kegg-database skill to search for KEGG compounds with the molecular formula C7H10N4O2 by using kegg_find with the formula option. Then retrieve the full entry details for the top result using kegg_get.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: A chemist wants to find KEGG compounds matching a specific molecular formula.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-step pathway enrichment workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the kegg-database skill to perform a pathway analysis workflow: (1) List all human pathways using kegg_list('pathway', 'hsa'), (2) For a list of gene IDs, use kegg_link to find their associated pathways, (3) Use kegg_get to retrieve detailed information for the enriched pathways, (4) Convert the gene IDs to UniProt identifiers using kegg_conv.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: A bioinformatician building a pathway enrichment pipeline needs to map multiple genes to pathways and convert identifiers.

prompt_templates.title: Drug interaction network analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the kegg-database skill to analyze drug-drug interactions. For a set of KEGG drug IDs (e.g., D00001, D00002, D00003), use kegg_ddi to retrieve known interactions. Then use kegg_get to retrieve detailed drug entries for each interacting drug to understand their mechanisms.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert: A pharmacologist wants to build an interaction network for a set of candidate drugs.

output_examples.input: Find pathways associated with human gene TP53 (hsa:7157) using kegg_link
output_examples.output: The skill returns a tab-delimited list of pathway IDs and names associated with TP53, including pathways in cancer (hsa05200), cellular senescence (hsa04218), apoptosis (hsa04210), and the p53 signaling pathway (hsa04115). Each line pairs a pathway ID with TP53's gene ID.

output_examples.input: Retrieve the glycolysis pathway entry for human using kegg_get('hsa00010')
output_examples.output: The skill returns the full KEGG pathway entry for glycolysis in humans, including the pathway name, description, list of all genes/enzymes in the pathway with their KO identifiers, and cross-references to related pathways, compounds, and modules.

best_practices: Batch requests by joining up to 10 entry IDs with '+' to minimize API calls, but keep image, kgml, and json requests to single entries only
Cache KEGG responses locally when running repeated queries to reduce API load and improve performance
Always check response content for error messages since the API returns text even for invalid queries
anti_patterns: Do not use kegg_get with the image option for multiple entries in a single call - it only supports one entry at a time
Do not send high-frequency concurrent requests to the KEGG API without backoff logic, as rate limits exist even if undocumented
Do not use this skill for commercial purposes without a KEGG license - the API is restricted to academic use

faq.question: Is the KEGG API free to use?
faq.answer: The KEGG REST API is provided free of charge for academic research use. Commercial and non-academic use requires a separate license from KEGG.

faq.question: What is the maximum number of entries I can request at once?
faq.answer: Most operations support up to 10 entries per request when joined with '+'. Format-specific options like image, kgml, and json are limited to a single entry per request.

faq.question: How do I convert KEGG gene IDs to UniProt IDs?
faq.answer: Use the kegg_conv function with target_db='uniprot' and source_db set to your KEGG gene ID, for example kegg_conv('uniprot', 'hsa:10458').

faq.question: Does this skill support batch pathway enrichment analysis?
faq.answer: This skill provides the data retrieval components for enrichment analysis (listing pathways, linking genes to pathways, getting pathway details), but statistical enrichment calculation must be performed separately.

faq.question: What output formats does kegg_get support?
faq.answer: kegg_get supports aaseq (FASTA amino acid), ntseq (FASTA nucleotide), mol (MOL format), kcf (KEGG Chemical Function), image (PNG), kgml (pathway XML), and json (pathway JSON).

faq.question: Can I use this skill to query non-human organisms?
faq.answer: Yes, KEGG supports many organisms using their three or four-letter codes such as mmu (mouse), dme (fruit fly), sce (yeast), and eco (E. coli). Use the organism code in your queries.
